title: To establish a Department of State Domestic Protection Mission relating to unmanned aircraft system and unmanned aircraft.
plain_summary: This bill gives the Secretary of State special powers to protect government buildings from dangerous drones. The Secretary can track, stop, take control of, or destroy drones that might be a threat to these facilities. When using these powers, the Secretary must work with airplane safety officials to make sure regular air travel stays safe and must protect people's privacy when listening to drone communications. The Secretary also has to report to Congress twice a year about how these drone-fighting powers are being used.
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Aviation.

sections.title: Section 1  Department of State Domestic Protection Mission
sections.text:
The Secretary of State can take action against drones that pose a threat to certain government facilities, even though other laws normally prevent this. The Secretary can let State Department security personnel and hired contractors with safety or security duties take these actions too. These actions are allowed when there is a real threat from a drone or drone system to the safety or security of a covered facility or asset. A covered facility is one that the Secretary identifies as high-risk for drone attacks. The Secretary must work with the Federal Aviation Administration to decide what counts as a real threat.

The Secretary and authorized personnel can do several things to stop dangerous drones. They can track and monitor drones while they are flying, including listening to the radio signals used to control them. They can warn the drone operator using various methods like electronic signals or radio messages. They can disrupt the drone's control system by interfering with its communication signals. They can take control of the drone or seize it completely. If needed, they can use reasonable force to disable, damage, or destroy the drone.

Before using any anti-drone technology, the Secretary must test and train with the equipment to make sure it works properly. Even personnel who do not normally handle security can help with this research and testing. The Secretary must coordinate with the Federal Aviation Administration before testing to make sure the testing does not interfere with safe airport operations or air traffic.

Any drone seized by the Secretary becomes property of the United States government under federal forfeiture laws. The Secretary of State and Secretary of Transportation can create rules and guidance for carrying out these actions. They must work with the National Telecommunications and Information Administration when making these rules.

The Secretary of State must coordinate closely with several agencies before taking any action. This includes working with the Federal Aviation Administration to make sure actions do not interfere with safe air travel or airport operations. The Secretary must also coordinate with the Federal Communications Commission and National Telecommunications and Information Administration when developing rules.

Any communications intercepted from drones must be handled carefully to protect privacy rights. The rules must follow the First and Fourth Amendments to the Constitution. Communications can only be intercepted when necessary for the allowed actions. Records of these communications can only be kept for one hundred eighty days unless they are needed for law enforcement investigations, ongoing security operations, or required by law. These communications cannot be shared outside the State Department unless needed for law enforcement, national security, or required by law.

Each year, the Secretary must include in the budget documents to Congress a report showing how much money is being spent on these anti-drone activities. This report can be mostly public but may include classified parts.

The Secretary can accept help, supplies, services, or money from other government agencies or private groups to carry out these anti-drone missions. The Secretary can also provide help to other agencies conducting similar missions when there are urgent circumstances, for a limited time and location.

Every six months for seven years after this law passes, the Secretary of State and Secretary of Transportation must brief Congress on how they are using these new powers. The briefings must cover policies to protect air travel, instances where actions were taken against drones, privacy protection procedures, how the public is informed about these authorities, coordination with law enforcement, and impacts on lawful drone operators. These briefings will be unclassified but may include classified portions.

This law does not give the Secretary of State any powers that belong to the Secretary of Transportation or Federal Aviation Administration, and vice versa. The authority to take these anti-drone actions expires seven years after this law is enacted.

The law defines several important terms. Appropriate congressional committees include the foreign affairs, intelligence, judiciary, and transportation committees in both the House and Senate. A covered facility is one identified as high-risk for drone attacks, located in the United States, and related to State Department security missions or personal protection duties. Personnel means State Department officers, employees, and contractors with security duties who are trained and certified for this work. Risk-based assessment means evaluating threats and potential impacts on air safety when deciding how to protect a facility. Unmanned aircraft and unmanned aircraft system refer to drones and their control systems as defined in federal aviation law.
